Challenge:
# Homesite Quote Conversion

## Competition Description
In the competitive world of homeowners insurance, predicting whether a customer will convert a quote into a purchase is vital for optimizing sales strategies and resource allocation. Homesite, a leading insurance provider, has shared anonymized data from over 260,000 quotes, each with nearly 300 features covering customer demographics, property details, and quote-specific fields. Your task is to build a model that predicts the probability of quote conversion, helping Homesite better understand customer behavior and improve targeting.

## Evaluation
### Metric
Submissions are evaluated using the area under the ROC curve (AUC-ROC) between the predicted probabilities and the true binary target indicating quote conversion. This metric measures the model's ability to distinguish between converters and non-converters across all classification thresholds.

Data description:
Homesite Quote Conversion

Dataset Description
This dataset captures the interactions of numerous customers interested in purchasing policies from Homesite. Each QuoteNumber represents a potential customer, while the QuoteConversion_Flag indicates whether a policy was purchased. The anonymized features encompass detailed coverage, sales, personal, property, and geographic information, providing a comprehensive view of prospective customers. Your objective is to predict the QuoteConversion_Flag for each QuoteNumber in the test set.

Challenge:
# Airbnb Recruiting New User Bookings

## Competition Description
Predict the first booking destination for new Airbnb users based on their user demographics and session activity. The dataset includes user information such as country of residence, affiliate tracking details, and web session logs, along with destination data for prior bookings. This task is crucial for personalizing recommendations, improving user experience, and increasing booking conversion rates in a global marketplace.

## Evaluation
### Metric
The evaluation metric is NDCG (Normalized Discounted Cumulative Gain) @5, which measures the quality of ranked predictions by assigning higher weight to correct destinations appearing in top positions. The score is normalized by the ideal DCG to account for varying relevance levels across users.

Data description:
Airbnb New User Bookings

Dataset Description
In this challenge, you will predict the first booking destination country for new users based on their demographics, web session records, and summary statistics. All users in this dataset are from the USA, with 12 possible destination outcomes: 'US', 'FR', 'CA', 'GB', 'ES', 'IT', 'PT', 'NL', 'DE', 'AU', 'NDF' (no destination found), and 'other'. 'NDF' indicates no booking occurred, while 'other' signifies a booking to a country not listed.

The training and test sets are divided by date, with the test set focusing on new users whose first activities occurred after 7/1/2014. The sessions dataset starts from 1/1/2014, while the users dataset dates back to 2010.

Files
- train_users.csv - the training set of users
- test_users.csv - the test set of users
- sessions.csv - web sessions log for users
- countries.csv - summary statistics of destination countries in this dataset and their locations
- age_gender_bkts.csv - summary statistics of users' age group, gender, country of destination
- sample_submission.csv - correct format for submitting your predictions

Available files:
  age_gender_bkts.csv (11.9 KB)
  countries.csv (632 B)
  sample_submission_NDF.csv (931.5 KB)
  sessions.csv (631.6 MB)
  test_users.csv (6.8 MB)
  train_users_2.csv (24.9 MB)

The dataset consists of multiple files including user metadata, session logs, and historical bookings, with features such as age, signup method, device, and browsing behavior, structured to support both classification and ranking approaches.

Challenge:
# Home Depot Product Search Relevance

## Competition Description
Home Depot is seeking Kagglers to enhance the shopping experience by developing a model that accurately predicts the relevance of search results. Customers expect quick and precise results for their home improvement needs, from simple tasks like installing a ceiling fan to complex renovations. Your task is to predict the relevance score of search queries relative to product descriptions using provided query-product pairs. This challenge offers a real-world application in e-commerce search ranking, where accurate relevance prediction improves user satisfaction and engagement.

## Evaluation
### Metric
Submissions are evaluated on the root mean squared error (RMSE), which measures the square root of the average squared differences between predicted and actual relevance scores. Lower RMSE values indicate better model performance, with perfect predictions yielding an RMSE of 0.

Data description:
Home Depot Product Search Relevance

Dataset Description
This dataset includes various products and actual customer search terms from Home Depot's website. The goal is to predict a relevance score for the combinations of search terms and products. Ground truth labels were created by crowdsourcing the search/product pairs to multiple human raters. Relevance scores range from 1 (not relevant) to 3 (highly relevant). For instance, "AA battery" is highly relevant to AA batteries (3), mildly relevant to a cordless drill battery (2), and not relevant to a snow shovel (1). Each pair was evaluated by at least three raters, and the provided scores are the average of their ratings. Raters had access to product images but not to attributes, and specific instructions for the raters are included in relevance_instructions.docx.

File descriptions
- train.csv - the training set, contains products, searches, and relevance scores
- test.csv - the test set, contains products and searches. You must predict the relevance for these pairs.
- product_descriptions.csv - contains a text description of each product. You may join this table to the training or test set via the product_uid.
- attributes.csv - provides extended information about a subset of the products (typically representing detailed technical specifications). Not every product will have attributes.
- sample_submission.csv - a file showing the correct submission format
- relevance_instructions.docx - the instructions provided to human raters

Data fields
- id - a unique Id field which represents a (search_term, product_uid) pair
- product_uid - an id for the products
- product_title - the product title
- product_description - the text description of the product (may contain HTML content)
- search_term - the search query
- relevance - the average of the relevance ratings for a given id
- name - an attribute name
- value - the attribute's value

Available files:
  attributes.csv (102.4 MB)
  product_descriptions.csv (105.8 MB)
  relevance_instructions.docx (107.5 KB)
  sample_submission.csv (1.4 MB)
  test.csv (17.7 MB)
  train.csv (8.3 MB)

sample_submission.csv columns: id, relevance
Sample:
id,relevance
1,1
4,1

The dataset includes search queries paired with product titles and descriptions, along with human-annotated relevance scores ranging from 1 to 3. Each row in the data represents a query-product pair, with additional metadata such as product attributes and search terms used to predict relevance.

Challenge:
# Facial Keypoints Detection

## Competition Description
In this challenge, you will predict the locations of key facial keypoints in grayscale images, such as eye centers, eyebrow ends, nose tip, and mouth corners. The dataset includes thousands of facial images with labeled coordinates for up to 15 keypoints, providing a realistic benchmark for facial landmark detection. This task is fundamental for applications in facial animation, emotion recognition, and human-computer interaction.

## Evaluation
### Metric
Submissions are evaluated using the mean squared error (MSE) between the predicted and true keypoint coordinates, averaged over all keypoints and images.

Data description:
Facial Keypoints Detection

Dataset Description
Each predicted keypoint is specified by an (x,y) real-valued pair in the space of pixel indices. There are 15 keypoints, which represent the following elements of the face:

left_eye_center, right_eye_center, left_eye_inner_corner, left_eye_outer_corner, right_eye_inner_corner, right_eye_outer_corner, left_eyebrow_inner_end, left_eyebrow_outer_end, right_eyebrow_inner_end, right_eyebrow_outer_end, nose_tip, mouth_left_corner, mouth_right_corner, mouth_center_top_lip, mouth_center_bottom_lip.

Left and right refer to the point of view of the subject. In some examples, some of the target keypoint positions are missing (encoded as missing entries in the csv, i.e., with nothing between two commas). The input image is given in the last field of the data files and consists of a list of pixels (ordered by row), as integers in (0,255). The images are 96x96 pixels.

Files
- training.csv: list of training images. Each row contains the (x,y) coordinates for 15 keypoints, and image data as a row-ordered list of pixels.
- test.csv: list of test images. Each row contains ImageId and image data as a row-ordered list of pixels.
- sample_submission.csv: list of keypoints to predict. Each row contains a RowId, ImageId, FeatureName, Location. FeatureName are "left_eye_center_x," "right_eyebrow_outer_end_y," etc. Location is what you need to predict.

Available files:
  IdLookupTable.csv (862.7 KB)
  SampleSubmission.csv (205.9 KB)
  test.csv (59.8 MB)
  training.csv (238.1 MB)

training.csv: ~7,049 rows, 31 columns
Columns (first 20): left_eye_center_x, left_eye_center_y, right_eye_center_x, right_eye_center_y, left_eye_inner_corner_x, left_eye_inner_corner_y, left_eye_outer_corner_x, left_eye_outer_corner_y, right_eye_inner_corner_x, right_eye_inner_corner_y, right_eye_outer_corner_x, right_eye_outer_corner_y, left_eyebrow_inner_end_x, left_eyebrow_inner_end_y, left_eyebrow_outer_end_x, left_eyebrow_outer_end_y, right_eyebrow_inner_end_x, right_eyebrow_inner_end_y, right_eyebrow_outer_end_x, right_eyebrow_outer_end_y, ... (11 more)

test.csv: ~1,783 rows, 2 columns

Each row in the dataset corresponds to a single face image, with 30 columns representing x and y coordinates for 15 facial keypoints, and the last column containing the raw pixel values as a space-separated string.

Challenge:
# Nyc Taxi Trip Duration

## Competition Description
Predict the total ride duration of taxi trips in New York City based on historical data from the NYC Taxi and Limousine Commission. The dataset includes features such as pickup and dropoff locations, timestamps, passenger count, and vendor information. This challenge offers insight into urban mobility patterns and the factors influencing travel time in a major metropolitan area.

## Evaluation
### Metric
Submissions are evaluated using the Root Mean Squared Logarithmic Error (RMSLE), which measures the ratio between predicted and actual trip durations on the logarithmic scale. This metric penalizes underestimates and overestimates equally and is well-suited for targets spanning several orders of magnitude.

Data description:
New York City Taxi Trip Duration

Dataset Description
The competition dataset is based on the 2016 NYC Yellow Cab trip record data made available in Big Query on Google Cloud Platform. The data was originally published by the NYC Taxi and Limousine Commission (TLC). The data was sampled and cleaned for the purposes of this playground competition. Based on individual trip attributes, participants should predict the duration of each trip in the test set.

Files
- train.csv - the training set (contains 1458644 trip records)
- test.csv - the testing set (contains 625134 trip records)
- sample_submission.csv - a sample submission file in the correct format

Data fields
- id - a unique identifier for each trip
- vendor_id - a code indicating the provider associated with the trip record
- pickup_datetime - date and time when the meter was engaged
- dropoff_datetime - date and time when the meter was disengaged
- passenger_count - the number of passengers in the vehicle (driver entered value)
- pickup_longitude - the longitude where the meter was engaged
- pickup_latitude - the latitude where the meter was engaged
- dropoff_longitude - the longitude where the meter was disengaged
- dropoff_latitude - the latitude where the meter was disengaged
- store_and_fwd_flag - This flag indicates whether the trip record was held in vehicle memory before sending to the vendor because the vehicle did not have a connection to the server - Y=store and forward; N=not a store and forward trip
- trip_duration - duration of the trip in seconds

Available files:
  sample_submission.csv (8.8 MB)
  test.csv (70.8 MB)
  train.csv (200.6 MB)

train.csv: ~1,458,644 rows, 11 columns
Columns: id, vendor_id, pickup_datetime, dropoff_datetime, passenger_count, pickup_longitude, pickup_latitude, dropoff_longitude, dropoff_latitude, store_and_fwd_flag, trip_duration

test.csv: ~625,134 rows, 9 columns

sample_submission.csv columns: id, trip_duration
Sample:
id,trip_duration
id3004672,959
id3505355,959

The training set includes trip duration and dropoff time, while the test set contains only pickup time and location; predictions must be made without knowledge of the future. Both datasets cover trips from the first half of 2016, with the test set including dates up to June 30.

Challenge:
# Bnp Paribas Cardif Claims Management

## Competition Description
In this competition, you are challenged to predict whether an insurance claim will be made based on a dataset of anonymized customer information provided by BNP Paribas Cardif. The dataset includes over 114,000 training examples with 132 anonymized features, along with a binary target indicating claim status. This real-world problem offers a chance to improve risk assessment in insurance, leveraging machine learning to handle high-dimensional, mixed-type data with missing values.

## Evaluation
### Metric
The evaluation metric is Log Loss, which measures the accuracy of predicted probabilities by penalizing incorrect classifications more heavily when the model is confident. Lower log loss indicates better-calibrated probability predictions.

Data description:
BNP Paribas Cardif Claims Management

Dataset Description
You are provided with an anonymized dataset containing both categorical and numeric variables available when the claims were received by BNP Paribas Cardif. All string type variables are categorical. There are no ordinal variables. The "target" column in the train set is the variable to predict. It is equal to 1 for claims suitable for an accelerated approval. The task is to predict a probability ("PredictedProb") for each claim in the test set.

Files
- train.csv - the training set including the target
- test.csv - the test set without the target
- sample_submission.csv - a sample submission file in the correct format

Available files:
  sample_submission.csv (1.2 MB)
  test.csv (115.7 MB)
  train.csv (115.9 MB)

train.csv: ~114,321 rows, 133 columns
Columns (first 20): ID, target, v1, v2, v3, v4, v5, v6, v7, v8, v9, v10, v11, v12, v13, v14, v15, v16, v17, v18, ... (113 more)

test.csv: ~114,393 rows, 132 columns

sample_submission.csv columns: ID, PredictedProb
Sample:
ID,PredictedProb
0,0.5
1,0.5

The data consists of mixed-type features (continuous, categorical, and binary) with missing values, and all identifying feature names and values have been anonymized for privacy. The training set includes an ID column, the target variable, and 131 anonymized features.

Challenge:
# Wsdm Cup Multilingual Chatbot Arena

## Competition Description
In this competition, participants are tasked with predicting which of two multilingual chatbot responses is preferred by users based on real-world human feedback. The dataset contains pairwise comparisons of model responses to prompts across multiple languages, collected from user interactions on a large-scale chatbot platform. Your goal is to build a model that accurately identifies the winning response, helping improve alignment of language models with human preferences.

## Evaluation
### Metric
Submissions will be evaluated based on categorization accuracy, which measures the percentage of correctly predicted winners in the pairwise comparisons.

Data description:
WSDM Cup - Multilingual Chatbot Arena

Dataset Description
The competition dataset comprises user interactions from the ChatBot Arena (formerly LMSYS). Each interaction features a judge providing a prompt to two different LLMs and indicating which model delivered the more satisfactory response. This is a Code Competition, and when your submission is scored, the example test data will be replaced with the full test set.

Files
train.parquet

- id - A unique string identifier for the row.
- prompt - The prompt that was given as an input to both models.
- response_[a/b] - The response from model_[a/b] to the given prompt.
- winner - The judge's selection. The ground truth target column.
- model_[a/b] - The identity of model_[a/b]. Only included in train.parquet.
- language - The language used in the prompt. Only included in train.parquet.

test.parquet

- id - A unique integer identifier for the row.
- prompt
- response_[a/b]
- scored - Whether or not the row is currently scored. During the model training phase this will be true for rows used for the public leaderboard; during the forecasting phase this will be true for rows used for the private leaderboard.

sample_submission.csv A submission file in the correct format.

- id
- winner

Note that the dataset for this competition contains text that may be considered profane, vulgar, or offensive.

Available files:
  sample_submission.csv (57 B)
  test.parquet (44.2 KB)
  train.parquet (113.9 MB)

sample_submission.csv columns: id, winner
Sample:
id,winner
327228,model_a
1139415,model_a

Each instance in the dataset includes a prompt, two model responses (model_a and model_b), and metadata such as language and model identifiers. The test set requires predictions for the preferred response (winner) given the interaction context.

Challenge:
# Allstate Claims Severity

## Competition Description
In this competition, you're challenged to predict the severity of insurance claims for Allstate, a leading U.S. insurer. The dataset contains anonymized categorical and continuous features representing policyholder and vehicle characteristics, with the goal of predicting the continuous loss amount for each claim. This real-world regression task offers insight into how machine learning can improve claims processing and customer experience in the insurance industry.

## Evaluation
### Metric
Submissions are evaluated using the mean absolute error (MAE), which measures the average absolute difference between the predicted and actual claim loss values. Lower MAE indicates better predictive accuracy.

Data description:
Allstate Claims Severity

Dataset Description
Each row in this dataset represents an insurance claim. You must predict the value for the 'loss' column. Variables prefaced with 'cat' are categorical, while those prefaced with 'cont' are continuous.

Files
- train.csv - the training set
- test.csv - the test set. You must predict the loss value for the ids in this file.
- sample_submission.csv - a sample submission file in the correct format

Available files:
  sample_submission.csv (1.1 MB)
  test.csv (47.2 MB)
  train.csv (72.4 MB)

train.csv: ~200,868 rows, 132 columns
Columns (first 20): id, cat1, cat2, cat3, cat4, cat5, cat6, cat7, cat8, cat9, cat10, cat11, cat12, cat13, cat14, cat15, cat16, cat17, cat18, cat19, ... (112 more)

test.csv: ~133,348 rows, 131 columns

sample_submission.csv columns: id, loss
Sample:
id,loss
4,0
6,0

The dataset consists of three files: 'train.csv' containing 200,868 rows with 132 columns including the target 'loss' column, 'test.csv' with 133,348 rows and 131 columns (excluding 'loss'), and 'sample_submission.csv' providing the correct submission format with 'id' and 'loss' columns. Features are labeled as 'cat' for categorical and 'cont' for continuous variables, and the task is to predict the continuous 'loss' value for each claim in the test set.

The dataset includes 116 categorical features and 14 continuous features, with a large number of sparse, high-cardinality categorical variables that pose interesting challenges for feature engineering and modeling.

Challenge:
# Yelp Recsys 2013

## Competition Description
Predict personalized business ratings for Yelp users in the Phoenix, AZ area as part of the 2013 Recommender Systems Challenge at ACM RecSys 2013. The dataset includes over 10,000 businesses and a rich collection of user reviews, ratings, and metadata, enabling the development of sophisticated recommendation models. This task offers a unique opportunity to work on real-world recommender systems using authentic user-generated content.

## Evaluation
### Metric
Submissions are evaluated using Root Mean Squared Error (RMSE), which measures the average difference between predicted and actual star ratings. Lower RMSE values indicate better predictive accuracy.

Data description:
RecSys2013: Yelp Business Rating Prediction

Dataset Description
The dataset provides a comprehensive overview of Yelp reviews, businesses, users, and check-ins specifically for the Phoenix, AZ metropolitan area. Participants can download either yelp_training_set.zip or yelp_training_set_mac.zip, as both contain the same files for compatibility.

Files
- Training set: yelp_training_set.zip
- Testing set: yelp_test_set.zip
- Sample submission format: sample_submission.csv

Available files:
  IdLookupTable.csv (1.2 MB)
  sampleSubmission.csv (516.9 KB)
  sampleSubmissionFinal.csv (910.1 KB)
  __MACOSX/ (3 items)
  final_test_set/ (4 items)
  yelp_test_set/ (5 items)
  yelp_training_set/ (9 items)

sampleSubmission.csv columns: RecommendationId, Stars
Sample:
RecommendationId,Stars
1,3.67452543988905
2,3.67452543988905

The dataset is organized into training and test directories containing JSON files for businesses, users, reviews, and check-ins. The IdLookupTable.csv maps RecommendationId to user and business IDs, specifying the user-business pairs for which predictions are required. Participants must predict star ratings for these pairs, with sampleSubmission.csv and sampleSubmissionFinal.csv indicating preliminary and final submission formats.

The data includes user reviews, business attributes, and star ratings, with the test set containing user-business pairs for which ratings must be predicted. The sample submission file provides the required format, listing RecommendationId and predicted Stars.

Challenge:
# Llm Classification Finetuning

## Competition Description
Large language models (LLMs) are becoming integral to our daily interactions, but ensuring their responses align with user preferences is essential for effective communication. This competition invites participants to predict which of two LLM-generated responses is preferred based on real-world human feedback data from Chatbot Arena. The dataset includes prompts, paired model responses, and human judgments, enabling you to train classifiers to accurately model human preferences.

## Evaluation
### Metric
Submissions are evaluated on the log loss between the predicted probabilities and the ground truth values, with label smoothing applied automatically (eps=auto) to improve robustness.

Data description:
LLM Classification Finetuning

Dataset Description
The competition dataset consists of user interactions from the ChatBot Arena. In each interaction, a judge provides prompts to two different LLMs and indicates which model delivered the more satisfactory response. The goal is to predict the judges' preferences and assess the likelihood of a given prompt/response pair being chosen as the winner. The dataset includes 55K rows in the training set, with an expected 25,000 rows in the test set.

Files
train.csv

- id - A unique identifier for the row.
- model_[a/b] - The identity of model_[a/b]. Included in train.csv but not test.csv.
- prompt - The prompt that was given as an input (to both models).
- response_[a/b] - The response from model_[a/b] to the given prompt.
- winner_model_[a/b/tie] - Binary columns marking the judge's selection. The ground truth target column.

test.csv

- id
- prompt
- response_[a/b]

sample_submission.csv A submission file in the correct format.

- id
- winner_model_[a/b/tie] - This is what is predicted from the test set.

Note: the dataset for this competition contains text that may be considered profane, vulgar, or offensive.

Available files:
  sample_submission.csv (237 B)
  test.csv (10.7 KB)
  train.csv (184.2 MB)

train.csv: ~57,477 rows, 9 columns
Columns: id, model_a, model_b, prompt, response_a, response_b, winner_model_a, winner_model_b, winner_tie

test.csv: ~3 rows, 4 columns

sample_submission.csv columns: id, winner_model_a, winner_model_b, winner_tie
Sample:
id,winner_model_a,winner_model_b,winner_tie
136060,0.3333333333333333,0.3333333333333333,0.3333333333333333
211333,0.3333333333333333,0.3333333333333333,0.3333333333333333

The dataset consists of paired model responses to user prompts, along with binary indicators showing whether Model A, Model B, or neither (tie) was preferred by human raters, enabling multi-class probabilistic classification.

description: Challenge: # Stumbleupon ## Competition Description In the StumbleUpon Evergreen Classification Challenge, you are tasked with building a machine learning model to classify web content as either 'ephemeral' (short-lived relevance) or 'evergreen' (long-lasting value). The dataset includes features derived from web pages such as text, metadata, and crawl information, along with binary labels indicating content longevity. This problem is crucial for improving content recommendation systems by prioritizing timeless, high-quality resources. ## Evaluation ### Metric Submissions are evaluated using the area under the ROC curve (AUC-ROC), which measures the model's ability to distinguish between evergreen and ephemeral content across all classification thresholds. A higher AUC indicates better performance in ranking positive instances above negative ones. Data description: StumbleUpon Evergreen Classification Challenge Dataset Description Note: researchers who wish to use this data outside the competition should download and read the data access agreement. There are two components to the data provided for this challenge: The first component is two files: train.tsv and test.tsv. Each is a tab-delimited text file containing the fields outlined below for 10,566 urls total. Fields for which no data is available are indicated with a question mark. - train.tsv is the training set and contains 7,395 urls. Binary evergreen labels (either evergreen (1) or non-evergreen (0)) are provided for this set. - test.tsv is the test/evaluation set and contains 3,171 urls. The second component is raw_content.zip, a zip file containing the raw content for each url, as seen by StumbleUpon's crawler. Each url's raw content is stored in a tab-delimited text file, named with the urlid as indicated in train.tsv and test.tsv. The following table includes field descriptions for train.tsv and test.tsv: | FieldName | Type | Description | | --- | --- | --- | | url | string | Url of the webpage to be classified | | urlid | integer | StumbleUpon's unique identifier for each url | | boilerplate | json | Boilerplate text | | alchemy_category | string | Alchemy category (per the publicly available Alchemy API found at www.alchemyapi.com) | | alchemy_category_score | double | Alchemy category score (per the publicly available Alchemy API found at www.alchemyapi.com) | | avglinksize | double | Average number of words in each link | | commonLinkRatio_1 | double | # of links sharing at least 1 word with 1 other links / # of links | | commonLinkRatio_2 | double | # of links sharing at least 1 word with 2 other links / # of links | | commonLinkRatio_3 | double | # of links sharing at least 1 word with 3 other links / # of links | | commonLinkRatio_4 | double | # of links sharing at least 1 word with 4 other links / # of links | | compression_ratio | double | Compression achieved on this page via gzip (measure of redundancy) | | embed_ratio | double | Count of number of usage | | frameBased | integer (0 or 1) | A page is frame-based (1) if it has no body markup but have a frameset markup | | frameTagRatio | double | Ratio of iframe markups over total number of markups | | hasDomainLink | integer (0 or 1) | True (1) if it contains an with an url with domain | | html_ratio | double | Ratio of tags vs text in the page | | image_ratio | double | Ratio of tags vs text in the page | | is_news | integer (0 or 1) | True (1) if StumbleUpon's news classifier determines that this webpage is news | | lengthyLinkDomain | integer (0 or 1) | True (1) if at least 3 's text contains more than 30 alphanumeric characters | | linkwordscore | double | Percentage of words on the page that are in hyperlink's text | | news_front_page | integer (0 or 1) | True (1) if StumbleUpon's news classifier determines that this webpage is front-page news | | non_markup_alphanum_characters | integer | Page's text's number of alphanumeric characters | | numberOfLinks | integer | Number of markups | | numwords_in_url | double | Number of words in url | | parametrizedLinkRatio | double | A link is parametrized if it's url contains parameters or has an attached onClick event | | spelling_errors_ratio | double | Ratio of words not found in wiki (considered to be a spelling mistake) | | label | integer (0 or 1) | User-determined label. Either evergreen (1) or non-evergreen (0); available for train.tsv only | Files The dataset includes the following files: - train.tsv - test.tsv - raw_content.zip - sampleSubmission.csv Available files: sampleSubmission.csv (22.0 KB) test.tsv (9.4 MB) train.tsv (22.0 MB) raw_content/ (10566 items) sampleSubmission.csv columns: urlid, label Sample: urlid,label 5865,0 782,0 The dataset includes a training set with labeled examples and an unlabeled test set; features encompass URL metadata, page text summaries, and third-party scores. Each row in the submission file corresponds to a urlid, with predictions representing the probability of the page being evergreen.

description:
Challenge:
# The Winton Stock Market Challenge

## Competition Description
Winton Capital invites data scientists to predict future stock returns using historical market data and anonymized features. The dataset includes thousands of rows with hundreds of features, including lagged returns and masked attributes, reflecting real-world challenges in financial forecasting. This competition offers a glimpse into the complexity of quantitative finance, where uncovering subtle, predictive signals in noisy, non-stationary data is key to success.

## Evaluation
### Metric
Submissions are evaluated using the Weighted Mean Absolute Error (WMAE), which measures the average absolute difference between predicted and actual returns, weighted by the importance of each sample. Lower WMAE indicates better predictive accuracy, with greater emphasis on higher-weighted predictions.

Data description:
The Winton Stock Market Challenge

Dataset Description
Updated 2015-12-21: Winton have added new data into the test set. If you downloaded the test set before 2015-12-21 please re-download the data set and submit predictions on this instead.

In this competition the challenge is to predict the return of a stock, given the history of the past few days.

We provide 5-day windows of time, days D-2, D-1, D, D+1, and D+2. You are given returns in days D-2, D-1, and part of day D, and you are asked to predict the returns in the rest of day D, and in days D+1 and D+2.

During day D, there is intraday return data, which are the returns at different points in the day. We provide 180 minutes of data, from t=1 to t=180. In the training set you are given the full 180 minutes, in the test set just the first 120 minutes are provided.

For each 5-day window, we also provide 25 features, Feature_1 to Feature_25. These may or may not be useful in your prediction.

Each row in the dataset is an arbitrary stock at an arbitrary 5 day time window.

How these returns are calculated is defined by Winton, and will not to be revealed to you in this competition. The data set is designed to be representative of real data and so should bring about a number of challenges.

File descriptions
- train.csv - the training set, including the columns of:
- test.csv - the test set, including the columns of:
- sample_submission.csv - a sample submission file in the correct format

Data fields
- Feature_1 to Feature_25: different features relevant to prediction
- Ret_MinusTwo:  this is the return from the close of trading on day D-2 to the close of trading on day D-1 (i.e. 1 day)
- Ret_MinusOne: this is the return from the close of trading on day D-1 to the point at which the intraday returns start on day D (approximately 1/2 day)
- Ret_2 to Ret_120: these are returns over approximately one minute on day D. Ret_2 is the return between t=1 and t=2.
- Ret_121 to Ret_180: intraday returns over approximately one minute on day D. These are the target variables you need to predict as {id}_{1-60}.
- Ret_PlusOne: this is the return from the time Ret_180 is measured on day D to the close of trading on day D+1. (approximately 1day). This is a target variable you need to predict as {id}_61.
- Ret_PlusTwo: this is the return from the close of trading on day D+1 to the close of trading on day D+2 (i.e. 1 day) This is a target variable you need to predict as {id}_62.
- Weight_Intraday: weight used to evaluate intraday return predictions Ret 121 to 180
- Weight_Daily: weight used to evaluate daily return predictions (Ret_PlusOne and Ret_PlusTwo).

  test_2.csv (288.5 MB)

Challenge:
# Facebook Recruiting Iv Human Or Bot

## Competition Description
In this competition, you'll help Facebook distinguish between human users and automated bots in online bidding systems by predicting the likelihood that a bidder is a robot. You're provided with anonymized user data including bidder IDs, payment accounts, and shipping addresses from past bidding sessions, with the goal of building a model that accurately identifies suspicious behavior. This challenge offers a real-world application of machine learning in fraud detection and user authentication, where even small improvements can significantly enhance platform security.

## Evaluation
### Metric
Submissions are evaluated using the area under the ROC curve (AUC-ROC), which measures the model's ability to distinguish between human and bot bidders across all classification thresholds. A higher AUC score indicates better performance in ranking positive instances (bots) higher than negative ones (humans).

Data description:
Facebook Recruiting IV: Human or Robot?

Dataset Description
This competition features two datasets: a bidder dataset containing bidder information (id, payment account, and address) and a bid dataset with 7.6 million bids made via mobile devices. The auction platform employs a fixed increment for bids, omitting specific bid amounts. Participants can analyze bidding behavior based on the timing of bids, auction details, or device used.

Files
- train.csv - the training set from the bidder dataset
- test.csv - the test set from the bidder dataset
- sampleSubmission.csv - a sample submission file in the correct format
- bids.csv - the bid dataset

Data fields
For the bidder dataset

- bidder_id – Unique identifier of a bidder.
- payment_account – Payment account associated with a bidder. These are obfuscated to protect privacy.
- address – Mailing address of a bidder. These are obfuscated to protect privacy.
- outcome – Label of a bidder indicating whether or not it is a robot. Value 1.0 indicates a robot, where value 0.0 indicates human. 
The outcome was half hand labeled, half stats-based. There are two types of "bots" with different levels of proof:

1. Bidders identified as bots/fraudulent with clear proof, whose accounts were banned by the auction site.
2. Bidders who may have just started their business or whose stats exceed the system-wide average, lacking clear proof of being bots.

For the bid dataset

- bid_id - unique id for this bid
- bidder_id – Unique identifier of a bidder (same as the bidder_id used in train.csv and test.csv)
- auction – Unique identifier of an auction
- merchandise – The category of the auction site campaign, indicating the bidder's search term or online advertisement.
- device – Phone model of a visitor
- time - Time that the bid is made (transformed to protect privacy).
- country - The country that the IP belongs to
- ip – IP address of a bidder (obfuscated to protect privacy).
- url - URL where the bidder was referred from (obfuscated to protect privacy).

Available files:
  bids.csv (925.2 MB)
  sampleSubmission.csv (197.4 KB)
  test.csv (535.8 KB)
  train.csv (237.6 KB)

train.csv: ~2,013 rows, 4 columns
Columns: bidder_id, payment_account, address, outcome

test.csv: ~4,700 rows, 3 columns

sampleSubmission.csv columns: bidder_id, prediction
Sample:
bidder_id,prediction
49bb5a3c944b8fc337981cc7a9ccae41u31d7,0.0
a921612b85a1494456e74c09393ccb65ylp4y,0.0

The training set includes labeled examples with an 'outcome' column indicating whether a bidder is a bot (1) or human (0), while the test set contains unlabeled instances for which participants must predict probabilities. All identifying information has been hashed to preserve privacy, making feature engineering and pattern recognition key to success.

Challenge:
# Flavours Of Physics Kernels Only

## Competition Description
In this challenge, you'll help uncover potential evidence of charged lepton flavour violation by identifying the rare decay τ → 3μ, a process not predicted by the Standard Model of particle physics. The dataset consists of simulated collision events with detailed kinematic and isolation variables, where your task is to classify whether a signal (τ → 3μ) or background process occurred. Discovering this decay could open new doors to understanding fundamental physics beyond current theories.

## Evaluation
### Metric
The evaluation uses the Weighted Area Under the ROC Curve, where different regions of the True Positive Rate (TPR) are assigned varying weights: TPR [0, 0.2] is weighted by 2.0, [0.2, 0.4] by 1.5, and the rest by 1.0, emphasizing early detection performance.

Data description:
Flavours of Physics: Finding τ  →  μμμ (Kernels Only)

Dataset Description
In this competition, you are given a list of collision events and their properties. You will then predict whether a τ → 3μ decay happened in this collision. This τ → 3μ is currently assumed by scientists not to happen, and the goal of this competition is to discover τ → 3μ happening more frequently than scientists currently can understand.

It is challenging to design a machine learning problem for something you have never observed before. Scientists at CERN developed the following designs to achieve the goal.

Available files:
  check_agreement.csv (254.4 MB)
  check_correlation.csv (4.2 MB)
  sample_submission.csv (23.4 MB)
  test.csv (638.8 MB)
  training.csv (53.1 MB)

training.csv: ~67,553 rows, 51 columns
Columns (first 20): id, LifeTime, dira, FlightDistance, FlightDistanceError, IP, IPSig, VertexChi2, pt, DOCAone, DOCAtwo, DOCAthree, IP_p0p2, IP_p1p2, isolationa, isolationb, isolationc, isolationd, isolatione, isolationf, ... (31 more)

test.csv: ~855,819 rows, 47 columns

sample_submission.csv columns: id, prediction
Sample:
id,prediction
14711831,0.5238745628382426
16316387,0.6294662739043109

The training data includes 67,553 events with 51 features, combining high-level physics variables and track-based observables, along with a signal flag and event ID. Features include particle lifetimes, isolation metrics, impact parameters, and reconstructed kinematics from collision vertices.

Challenge:
# Linking Writing Processes To Writing Quality

## Competition Description
This competition challenges participants to link writing processes to writing quality by analyzing keystroke log data collected from writers composing essays. The dataset includes detailed behavioral traces such as typing dynamics, pauses, revisions, and navigation patterns, allowing for deep exploration of how cognitive strategies like planning and editing influence final essay quality. By predicting human-assessed writing scores from process data, this task offers a unique opportunity to uncover measurable behavioral indicators of effective writing.

## Evaluation
### Metric
Submissions are evaluated using Root Mean Squared Error (RMSE), which measures the average magnitude of prediction errors between predicted and actual writing scores. Lower RMSE values indicate better model performance, with a perfect score being 0.

Data description:
Linking Writing Processes to Writing Quality

Dataset Description
The competition dataset consists of approximately 5000 logs capturing user inputs, including keystrokes and mouse clicks, during essay composition. Each essay is scored on a scale from 0 to 6, and the objective is to predict the score based on the corresponding log data. More details about the dataset can be found on the Data Collection Procedure page.

Files
- train_logs.csv - Input logs for training. Alphanumeric characters are anonymized as "q" to protect essay text, while punctuation remains unchanged.
  - id - Unique essay ID
  - event_id - Chronological event index
  - down_time - Time of the down event (ms)
  - up_time - Time of the up event (ms)
  - action_time - Duration of the event (up_time - down_time)
  - activity - Event category
  - Nonproduction - No text alteration
  - Input - Text added to the essay
  - Remove/Cut - Text removed from the essay
  - Paste - Text changed via paste
  - Replace - Text replaced with another string
  - Move From [x1, y1] To [x2, y2] - Text moved to a new location
  - down_event - Key/mouse press event name
  - up_event - Key/mouse release event name
  - text_change - Text change resulting from the event
  - cursor_position - Cursor index after the event
  - word_count - Essay word count after the event

- test_logs.csv - Input logs for testing, with the same fields as train_logs.csv. Public version contains example logs only.
- train_scores.csv
  - id - Unique essay ID
  - score - Essay score out of 6 (prediction target)
- sample_submission.csv - Submission file in the correct format. Refer to the Evaluation page for details.

Available files:
  sample_submission.csv (48 B)
  test_logs.csv (398 B)
  train_logs.csv (485.7 MB)
  train_scores.csv (32.1 KB)

sample_submission.csv columns: id, score
Sample:
id,score
0000aaaa,1.0
2222bbbb,2.0

The dataset includes timestamped keystroke logs and derived behavioral features for each writing session, aggregated into a tabular format per essay. Each row in the data corresponds to a unique writing session, identified by an 'id', with the target 'score' representing the human-rated essay quality.

Challenge:
# Grupo Bimbo Inventory Demand

## Competition Description
In this competition, you're challenged to predict the demand for over 1 million products sold by Grupo Bimbo across thousands of stores in Mexico. The dataset includes historical sales, product, client, and route information, with the goal of accurately forecasting daily demand to optimize inventory and reduce waste. This real-world problem highlights the importance of supply chain efficiency in the fast-moving consumer goods industry.

## Evaluation
### Metric
The evaluation metric is Root Mean Squared Logarithmic Error (RMSLE), which measures the ratio between predicted and actual demand on a logarithmic scale, penalizing under- and over-predictions equally while being robust to differences in scale.

Data description:
Grupo Bimbo Inventory Demand

Dataset Description
In this competition, you will forecast the demand for a product at a specific store for a given week. The dataset includes 9 weeks of sales transactions in Mexico, with each transaction representing sales and returns. Demand for a product in a week is calculated as the sales for that week minus the returns for the following week.

The train and test datasets are time-split, along with public and private leaderboard datasets.

Key points to note:

- Some products in the test set may not appear in the train set, reflecting the dynamic nature of inventory data. Your model should adapt to this.
- Duplicate Cliente_IDs in cliente_tabla indicate that one Cliente_ID may correspond to multiple similar NombreCliente entries, necessitating data cleaning.
- Adjusted demand (Demanda_uni_equil) is always >= 0, as demand cannot be negative. Negative values for Venta_uni_hoy - Dev_uni_proxima may occur due to returns spanning multiple weeks.

File descriptions
- train.csv — the training set
- test.csv — the test set
- sample_submission.csv — a sample submission file in the correct format
- cliente_tabla.csv — client names (can be joined with train/test on Cliente_ID)
- producto_tabla.csv — product names (can be joined with train/test on Producto_ID)
- town_state.csv — town and state (can be joined with train/test on Agencia_ID)

Data fields
- Semana — Week number (From Thursday to Wednesday)
- Agencia_ID — Sales Depot ID
- Canal_ID — Sales Channel ID
- Ruta_SAK — Route ID (Several routes = Sales Depot)
- Cliente_ID — Client ID
- NombreCliente — Client name
- Producto_ID — Product ID
- NombreProducto — Product Name
- Venta_uni_hoy — Sales unit this week (integer)
- Venta_hoy — Sales this week (unit: pesos)
- Dev_uni_proxima — Returns unit next week (integer)
- Dev_proxima — Returns next week (unit: pesos)
- Demanda_uni_equil — Adjusted Demand (integer) (This is the target you will predict)

Available files:
  cliente_tabla.csv (21.3 MB)
  producto_tabla.csv (109.5 KB)
  sample_submission.csv (68.9 MB)
  test.csv (251.1 MB)
  town_state.csv (29.2 KB)
  train.csv (3.2 GB)

train.csv: ~74,180,464 rows, 11 columns
Columns: Semana, Agencia_ID, Canal_ID, Ruta_SAK, Cliente_ID, Producto_ID, Venta_uni_hoy, Venta_hoy, Dev_uni_proxima, Dev_proxima, Demanda_uni_equil

test.csv: ~6,999,251 rows, 7 columns

sample_submission.csv columns: id, Demanda_uni_equil
Sample:
id,Demanda_uni_equil
0,7
1,7

The training data spans multiple weeks with detailed transaction records, while the test set requires predictions for future time periods, emphasizing temporal forecasting. Each row represents a product-store-week combination with hierarchical categorical features and continuous sales variables.

- Adjusted demand (Demanda_uni_equil) is always >= 0, as demand cannot be negative. Negative values for Venta_uni_hoy - Dev_uni_proxima may occur due to returns spanning multiple weeks.

File descriptions
- train.csv — the training set
- test.csv — the test set
- sample_submission.csv — a sample submission file in the correct format
- cliente_tabla.csv — client names (can be joined with train/test on Cliente_ID)
- producto_tabla.csv — product names (can be joined with train/test on Producto_ID)
- town_state.csv — town and state (can be joined with train/test on Agencia_ID)

Data fields
- Semana — Week number (From Thursday to Wednesday)
- Agencia_ID — Sales Depot ID
- Canal_ID — Sales Channel ID
- Ruta_SAK — Route ID (Several routes = Sales Depot)
- Cliente_ID — Client ID
- NombreCliente — Client name
- Producto_ID — Product ID
- NombreProducto — Product Name
- Venta_uni_hoy — Sales unit this week (integer)
- Venta_hoy — Sales this week (unit: pesos)
- Dev_uni_proxima — Returns unit next week (integer)
- Dev_proxima — Returns next week (unit: pesos)
- Demanda_uni_equil — Adjusted Demand (integer) (This is the target you will predict)

Available files:
  cliente_tabla.csv (21.3 MB)
  producto_tabla.csv (109.5 KB)
  sample_submission.csv (68.9 MB)
  test.csv (251.1 MB)
  town_state.csv (29.2 KB)
  train.csv (3.2 GB)

Columns: Semana, Agencia_ID, Canal_ID, Ruta_SAK, Cliente_ID, Producto_ID, Venta_uni_hoy, Venta_hoy, Dev_uni_proxima, Dev_proxima, Demanda_uni_equil

test.csv: ~6,999,251 rows, 7 columns

sample_submission.csv columns: id, Demanda_uni_equil

id,Demanda_uni_equil
0,7

The training data spans multiple weeks with detailed transaction records, while the test set requires predictions for future time periods, emphasizing temporal forecasting. Each row represents a product-store-week combination with hierarchical categorical features and continuous sales variables.

Challenge:
# Quora Question Pairs

## Competition Description
In this competition, you are tasked with identifying whether pairs of questions from Quora are duplicates of each other. The dataset includes over 400,000 question pairs from Quora, each labeled as either duplicate or not, along with a much larger set of unlabeled pairs for testing. This problem is important for improving content discovery and reducing redundancy on knowledge-sharing platforms, making it a valuable natural language processing challenge.

## Evaluation
### Metric
Submissions are evaluated using log loss (logarithmic loss), which measures the accuracy of predicted probabilities. Lower log loss values indicate better calibration of the predicted probabilities against the true binary labels.

Data description:
Quora Question Pairs

Dataset Description
The goal of this competition is to predict which pairs of questions have the same meaning. Ground truth labels, provided by human experts, are subjective and may not always be accurate due to the inherent ambiguity in language. While these labels represent a reasonable consensus, they can include errors on a case-by-case basis.

To prevent cheating, the test set includes computer-generated question pairs that are not from Quora and will not be scored. All training set questions are authentic examples from Quora.

Data fields
- id - the id of a training set question pair
- qid1, qid2 - unique ids of each question (only available in train.csv)
- question1, question2 - the full text of each question
- is_duplicate - the target variable, set to 1 if question1 and question2 have essentially the same meaning, and 0 otherwise.

Files
- train.csv - the training set containing question pairs and labels
- test.csv - the test set containing question pairs for evaluation
- sample_submission.csv - a sample submission file format

Available files:
  sample_submission.csv (22.3 MB)
  test.csv (477.6 MB)
  train.csv (63.4 MB)

train.csv: ~404,301 rows, 6 columns
Columns: id, qid1, qid2, question1, question2, is_duplicate

test.csv: ~3,563,489 rows, 3 columns

sample_submission.csv columns: test_id, is_duplicate
Sample:
test_id,is_duplicate
0,1
1,1

The training set contains question pairs with unique identifiers and a duplicate label, while the test set includes only question pairs with test IDs, requiring predictions of duplicate probabilities. The large size of the test set reflects the real-world scale of detecting redundancy across millions of user-generated questions.

Challenge:
# Bigquery Geotab Intersection Congestion

## Competition Description
In this competition, participants will predict traffic congestion at urban intersections using real-world telematics data from commercial vehicles. The dataset includes aggregated traffic patterns such as time and distance between stops, along with location and time metadata, collected across multiple cities. By accurately forecasting congestion, solutions can help improve urban mobility, reduce commute times, and inform smarter transportation planning.

## Evaluation
### Metric
Submissions are evaluated using the root mean squared error (RMSE), which measures the average magnitude of prediction errors between actual and predicted congestion values. Lower RMSE scores indicate better model performance, with perfect predictions achieving a score of 0.

Data description:
BigQuery-Geotab Intersection Congestion

Dataset Description
UPDATE: This dataset is no longer available via BigQuery. However, you can source it directly from the Kaggle dataset on this competition.

The data consists of aggregated trip logging metrics from commercial vehicles, such as semi-trucks. The data have been grouped by intersection, month, hour of day, direction driven through the intersection, and whether the day was on a weekend or not.

For each grouping in the test set, you need to make predictions for three different quantiles of two different metrics covering how long it took the group of vehicles to drive through the intersection. Specifically, the 20th, 50th, and 80th percentiles for the total time stopped at an intersection and the distance between the intersection and the first place a vehicle stopped while waiting. You can think of your goal as summarizing the distribution of wait times and stop distances at each intersection.

Each of those six predictions goes on a new row in the submission file. Read the submission TargetId fields, such as 1_1, as the first number being the RowId and the second being the metric id. You can unpack the submission metric id codes with submission_metric_map.json.

The training set includes an optional additional output metric (TimeFromFirstStop) in case you find that useful for building your models. It was only excluded from the test set to limit the number of predictions that must be made.

The instructions in BigQuery-Dataset-Access.md are now deprecated. The dataset is only available through Kaggle, via download below or API.

Files
The dataset is available for download on Kaggle. Please refer to the competition page for the specific files included.

Available files:
  BigQuery-Dataset-Access.md (893 B)
  sample_submission.csv (131.6 MB)
  submission_metric_map (183 B)
  submission_metric_map.json (183 B)
  test.csv (269.1 MB)
  train.csv (177.2 MB)

train.csv: ~856,387 rows, 28 columns
Columns: RowId, IntersectionId, Latitude, Longitude, EntryStreetName, ExitStreetName, EntryHeading, ExitHeading, Hour, Weekend, Month, Path, TotalTimeStopped_p20, TotalTimeStopped_p40, TotalTimeStopped_p50, TotalTimeStopped_p60, TotalTimeStopped_p80, TimeFromFirstStop_p20, TimeFromFirstStop_p40, TimeFromFirstStop_p50, TimeFromFirstStop_p60, TimeFromFirstStop_p80, DistanceToFirstStop_p20, DistanceToFirstStop_p40, DistanceToFirstStop_p50, DistanceToFirstStop_p60, DistanceToFirstStop_p80, City

test.csv: ~1,921,357 rows, 13 columns

sample_submission.csv columns: TargetId, Target
Sample:
TargetId,Target
0_0,0
0_1,0

The training data contains detailed congestion metrics at various percentiles for each intersection, while the test set includes only the contextual features, requiring participants to predict the congestion levels. Each row represents a unique traffic movement pattern defined by entry and exit streets, direction, time, and location.

Challenge:
# Loan Default Prediction

## Competition Description
This competition challenges participants to predict the expected loss from loan defaults by estimating both the probability of default and the severity of potential losses. You are provided with historical loan data including borrower characteristics and loan performance, and your task is to predict the continuous loss value for each loan. This approach moves beyond simple binary classification, offering a more realistic and financially meaningful assessment of credit risk.

## Evaluation
### Metric
Submissions are evaluated using the mean absolute error (MAE), which measures the average absolute difference between predicted and actual loss values. Lower MAE indicates better performance, with perfect predictions achieving a score of 0.

Data description:
Loan Default Prediction - Imperial College London

Dataset Description
This dataset contains financial transaction records for individuals, featuring over 200,000 observations and nearly 800 features. Each observation is independent, indicating whether a default occurred and measuring the loss, which ranges from 0 to 100. The loss is normalized based on a notional value of 100, meaning a loss of 60 indicates a reimbursement of 40. If there was no default, the loss is 0. Missing feature values are retained to allow teams to utilize the full dataset and implement their own strategies for handling gaps. Some variables may be categorical (e.g., f776 and f777). The data has been standardized, de-trended, and anonymized, with the competition sponsor ensuring the removal of time-dimensionality, although training set observations are ordered chronologically while the test set is randomized.

Files
The files included in this competition are:

- train.csv
- test.csv
- sample_submission.csv

Available files:
  sampleSubmission.csv (1.9 MB)
  test_v2.csv (1.0 GB)
  train_v2.csv (511.0 MB)

sampleSubmission.csv columns: id, loss
Sample:
id,loss
105472,0
105473,0

The dataset is split into training and test sets: 'train_v2.csv' contains labeled observations with features and the 'loss' target, while 'test_v2.csv' contains similar features but without labels, requiring participants to predict the 'loss' for each id. The 'sampleSubmission.csv' file demonstrates the required submission format, which includes the 'id' from the test set and the predicted 'loss' value.

The dataset includes a training set with features describing loan and borrower attributes, and a test set for which participants must predict the 'loss' value. The sample submission file contains one row for each test instance, with columns 'id' and 'loss'.

Challenge:
# Global Wheat Detection

## Competition Description
Detecting wheat heads in outdoor field images is a critical task for estimating yield and monitoring crop health. In this competition, you'll build a model to detect wheat heads by predicting bounding boxes in images captured from diverse wheat fields around the world. The dataset includes images from multiple sources, varying in geography, imaging conditions, and wheat varieties, making it a challenging and realistic computer vision task.

## Evaluation
### Metric
The competition is evaluated using mean average precision (mAP) across a range of Intersection over Union (IoU) thresholds, from 0.5 to 0.75 in 0.05 increments. A prediction is considered correct if its IoU with a ground truth box exceeds the current threshold, and the metric aggregates precision across all classes and thresholds.

Data description:
Global Wheat Detection 

Dataset Description
The dataset consists of images of wheat fields with bounding boxes for each identified wheat head. Not all images contain wheat heads or bounding boxes. The images were captured from various locations worldwide. The CSV data includes the image ID, width, height, and bounding box information. Each row in train.csv corresponds to a bounding box, and not all images have bounding boxes. Most test set images are hidden, but a small subset is available for coding purposes.

Files
- train.csv - the training data
- sample_submission.csv - a sample submission file in the correct format
- train.zip - training images
- test.zip - test images

Available files:
  sample_submission.csv (266 B)
  train.csv (8.3 MB)
  test/ (10 items)
  train/ (3422 items)

train.csv: ~147,793 rows, 5 columns
Columns: image_id, width, height, bbox, source

sample_submission.csv columns: image_id, PredictionString
Sample:
image_id,PredictionString
aac893a91,1.0 0 0 50 50
51f1be19e,1.0 0 0 50 50

The training data consists of images with associated bounding boxes stored in a CSV file, where each row corresponds to a single wheat head annotation. The test set contains images without annotations, and submissions require predicted bounding boxes in a specific format including confidence scores.

Challenge:
# Pubg Finish Placement Prediction

## Competition Description
Predict a player's final placement in a PUBG match based on their in-game statistics and actions. You'll be provided with a dataset of over 65,000 anonymized match instances, each containing detailed player-level features such as kills, damage dealt, movement, and team interactions. This challenge offers a fascinating look into player behavior and performance in one of the most popular battle royale games, testing your ability to extract meaningful patterns from complex, real-world gaming data.

## Evaluation
### Metric
Submissions are evaluated using Mean Absolute Error (MAE) between the predicted and actual winPlacePerc values, where winPlacePerc represents a player's finishing position as a normalized percentage (0 = last place, 1 = first place). Lower MAE indicates better predictive accuracy.

Data description:
PUBG Finish Placement Prediction (Kernels Only)

Dataset Description
In a PUBG match, up to 100 players compete, either solo or in teams, with their performance ranked based on survival. The dataset includes anonymized game stats, where each row represents a player's post-game performance. Matches can vary in player count and team size, and your goal is to create a model that predicts players' finishing placements on a scale from 1 (first place) to 0 (last place).

File descriptions
- train_V2.csv - the training set
- test_V2.csv - the test set
- sample_submission_V2.csv - a sample submission file in the correct format

Data fields
- DBNOs -  Number of enemy players knocked.
- assists -  Number of enemy players this player damaged that were killed by teammates.
- boosts -  Number of boost items used.
- damageDealt -  Total damage dealt. Note: Self inflicted damage is subtracted.
- headshotKills - Number of enemy players killed with headshots.
- heals - Number of healing items used.
- Id - Player’s Id
- killPlace -  Ranking in match of number of enemy players killed.
- killPoints - Kills-based external ranking of player. (Think of this as an Elo ranking where only kills matter.) If there is a value other than -1 in rankPoints, then any 0 in killPoints should be treated as a “None”.
- killStreaks - Max number of enemy players killed in a short amount of time.
- kills - Number of enemy players killed.
- longestKill - Longest distance between player and player killed at time of death. This may be misleading, as downing a player and driving away may lead to a large longestKill stat.
- matchDuration - Duration of match in seconds.
- matchId - ID to identify match. There are no matches that are in both the training and testing set.
- matchType - String identifying the game mode that the data comes from. The standard modes are “solo”, “duo”, “squad”, “solo-fpp”, “duo-fpp”, and “squad-fpp”; other modes are from events or custom matches.
- rankPoints - Elo-like ranking of player. This ranking is inconsistent and is being deprecated in the API’s next version, so use with caution. Value of -1 takes place of “None”.
- revives - Number of times this player revived teammates.
- rideDistance - Total distance traveled in vehicles measured in meters.
- roadKills - Number of kills while in a vehicle.
- swimDistance - Total distance traveled by swimming measured in meters.
- teamKills - Number of times this player killed a teammate.
- vehicleDestroys - Number of vehicles destroyed.
- walkDistance - Total distance traveled on foot measured in meters.
- weaponsAcquired - Number of weapons picked up.
- winPoints - Win-based external ranking of player. (Think of this as an Elo ranking where only winning matters.) If there is a value other than -1 in rankPoints, then any 0 in winPoints should be treated as a “None”.
- groupId - ID to identify a group within a match. If the same group of players plays in different matches, they will have a different groupId each time.
- numGroups - Number of groups we have data for in the match.
- maxPlace - Worst placement we have data for in the match. This may not match with numGroups, as sometimes the data skips over placements.
- winPlacePerc - The target of prediction. This is a percentile winning placement, where 1 corresponds to 1st place, and 0 corresponds to last place in the match. It is calculated off of maxPlace, not numGroups, so it is possible to have missing chunks in a match.

Available files:
  sample_submission_V2.csv (32.9 MB)
  test_V2.csv (273.2 MB)
  train_V2.csv (659.5 MB)

Each row represents a player in a match, with features including both individual and team-level statistics, and the target winPlacePerc reflects the player’s relative finishing position. Matches can contain between 1 and 100 players, and multiple rows correspond to the same match ID.

- winPoints - Win-based external ranking of player. (Think of this as an Elo ranking where only winning matters.) If there is a value other than -1 in rankPoints, then any 0 in winPoints should be treated as a “None”.
- groupId - ID to identify a group within a match. If the same group of players plays in different matches, they will have a different groupId each time.
- numGroups - Number of groups we have data for in the match.
- maxPlace - Worst placement we have data for in the match. This may not match with numGroups, as sometimes the data skips over placements.
- winPlacePerc - The target of prediction. This is a percentile winning placement, where 1 corresponds to 1st place, and 0 corresponds to last place in the match. It is calculated off of maxPlace, not numGroups, so it is possible to have missing chunks in a match.

Available files:
  sample_submission_V2.csv (32.9 MB)
  test_V2.csv (273.2 MB)
  train_V2.csv (659.5 MB)

Each row represents a player in a match, with features including both individual and team-level statistics, and the target winPlacePerc reflects the player’s relative finishing position. Matches can contain between 1 and 100 players, and multiple rows correspond to the same match ID.

Challenge:
# Avazu Ctr Prediction

## Competition Description
In online advertising, predicting whether a user will click on an ad is essential for optimizing ad placement and maximizing revenue. This competition challenges participants to build a model that predicts the probability of a click for each ad impression using 11 days of real-world data from Avazu's mobile advertising platform. The dataset includes anonymized features representing devices, apps, sites, and user behavior, making it a realistic and engaging benchmark for CTR prediction.

## Evaluation
### Metric
Submissions are evaluated using Logarithmic Loss (log loss), which measures the accuracy of predicted probabilities by penalizing incorrect classifications more heavily when the predicted probability is confident and wrong. Lower log loss values indicate better model performance.

Data description:
Click-Through Rate Prediction

Dataset Description
- **train** - Training set. 10 days of click-through data, ordered chronologically. Non-clicks and clicks are subsampled according to different strategies.
- **test** - Test set. 1 day of ads to for testing your model predictions.
- **sampleSubmission.csv** - Sample submission file in the correct format, corresponds to the All-0.5 Benchmark.

Data fields
- id: ad identifier
- click: 0/1 for non-click/click
- hour: format is YYMMDDHH, so 14091123 means 23:00 on Sept. 11, 2014 UTC.
- C1 -- anonymized categorical variable
- banner_pos
- site_id
- site_domain
- site_category
- app_id
- app_domain
- app_category
- device_id
- device_ip
- device_model
- device_type
- device_conn_type
- C14-C21 -- anonymized categorical variables

Files
- **train** - Training set. 10 days of click-through data, ordered chronologically. Non-clicks and clicks are subsampled according to different strategies.
- **test** - Test set. 1 day of ads to for testing your model predictions.
- **sampleSubmission.csv** - Sample submission file in the correct format, corresponds to the All-0.5 Benchmark.

Available files:
  sampleSubmission.gz (34.8 MB)
  test.gz (123.8 MB)
  train.gz (1.1 GB)

Each row in the dataset represents a single ad impression with a timestamp, anonymized categorical features, and a binary 'click' label indicating if the ad was clicked. The data is ordered by time, enabling time-based train-test splits for realistic evaluation.

Challenge:
# Plant Seedlings Classification

## Competition Description
Can you distinguish between weed and crop seedlings using images? This dataset, provided by Aarhus University Signal Processing group in collaboration with the University of Southern Denmark, contains labeled images of plant seedlings, enabling machine learning models to support precision agriculture by identifying species early in growth. Accurate classification can help optimize farming practices, reduce herbicide use, and improve crop yields.

## Evaluation
### Metric
Submissions are evaluated using MeanFScore, which at Kaggle refers to the micro-averaged F1-score. This metric aggregates the contributions of all classes to compute overall precision and recall, making it well-suited for multi-class classification with potential class imbalances.

Data description:
Plant Seedlings Classification

Dataset Description
You are provided with a training set and a test set of images of plant seedlings at various growth stages. Each image has a filename that serves as its unique ID. The dataset includes 12 plant species, and the competition's goal is to develop a classifier that can identify a plant's species from a photo. The species list is as follows:

```
Black-grass
Charlock
Cleavers
Common Chickweed
Common wheat
Fat Hen
Loose Silky-bent
Maize
Scentless Mayweed
Shepherds Purse
Small-flowered Cranesbill
Sugar beet
```

File descriptions
- train.csv - the training set, with plant species organized by folder
- test.csv - the test set, you need to predict the species of each image
- sample_submission.csv - a sample submission file in the correct format

Available files:
  sample_submission.csv (19.9 KB)
  test/ (794 items)
  train/ (12 items)

sample_submission.csv columns: file, species
Sample:
file,species
0021e90e4.png,Sugar beet
003d61042.png,Sugar beet

The dataset includes images of various plant seedlings, with filenames corresponding to entries in the sample submission file. Each row in the submission requires the predicted species for a given image file.

Challenge:
# Shopee Product Matching

## Competition Description
In this competition, you are tasked with identifying duplicate products across a large dataset of online marketplace listings. Given images and titles of products, your model must group together all postings that refer to the same product using the provided image, image hash, and title data. This problem is crucial for e-commerce platforms to maintain pricing competitiveness and improve product discovery by eliminating redundancy in search results.

## Evaluation
### Metric
Submissions are evaluated using the mean F1 score, computed in a sample-wise manner: for each row, the F1 score between the predicted matches and the ground truth is calculated, then averaged across all samples.

Data description:
Shopee - Price Match Guarantee

Dataset Description
Finding near-duplicates in large datasets is an important problem for many online businesses. In Shopee's case, everyday users can upload their own images and write their own product descriptions, adding an extra layer of challenge. Your task is to identify which products have been posted repeatedly. The differences between related products may be subtle while photos of identical products may be wildly different!

As this is a code competition, only the first few rows/images of the test set are published; the remainder are only available to your notebook when it is submitted. Expect to find roughly 70,000 images in the hidden test set. The few test rows and images that are provided are intended to illustrate the hidden test set format and folder structure.

Files
[train/test].csv - the training set metadata. Each row contains the data for a single posting. Multiple postings might have the exact same image ID, but with different titles or vice versa.

- posting_id - the ID code for the posting.
- image - the image id/md5sum.
- image_phash - a perceptual hash of the image.
- title - the product description for the posting.
- label_group - ID code for all postings that map to the same product. Not provided for the test set.

[train/test]images - the images associated with the postings.

sample_submission.csv - a sample submission file in the correct format.

- posting_id - the ID code for the posting.
- matches - Space delimited list of all posting IDs that match this posting. Posts always self-match. Group sizes were capped at 50, so there's no need to predict more than 50 matches.

Available files:
  sample_submission.csv (115 B)
  test.csv (456 B)
  train.csv (4.8 MB)
  test_images/ (3 items)
  train_images/ (32412 items)

train.csv: ~34,250 rows, 5 columns
Columns: posting_id, image, image_phash, title, label_group

test.csv: ~3 rows, 4 columns

sample_submission.csv columns: posting_id, matches
Sample:
posting_id,matches
test_2255846744,test_2255846744
test_3588702337,test_3588702337

Each product posting includes an image, a perceptual hash (image_phash) for image similarity, and a title. The training set includes a label_group column that identifies which postings correspond to the same product.

Challenge:
# The Nature Conservancy Fisheries Monitoring

## Competition Description
The Nature Conservancy is addressing the global challenge of illegal, unreported, and unregulated fishing by leveraging computer vision to monitor fishing activity in the Western and Central Pacific, where 60% of the world’s tuna is caught. Participants are tasked with classifying fish species and identifying fishing vessels from images captured by onboard cameras. The dataset includes labeled images from various fishing trips, enabling the development of models that can support sustainable fisheries management and protect marine ecosystems.

## Evaluation
### Metric
Submissions are evaluated using multi-class logarithmic loss, which measures the accuracy of predicted class probabilities. The lower the log loss, the better the model’s predicted probabilities match the true labels.

Data description:
The Nature Conservancy Fisheries Monitoring

Dataset Description
Warning: This data contains graphic contents that some may find disturbing. Please note that this competition is governed by the Competition Rules and this NDA.

In this competition, The Nature Conservancy asks you to help them detect which species of fish appears on a fishing boat, based on images captured from boat cameras of various angles.

Your goal is to predict the likelihood of fish species in each picture.

Eight target categories are available in this dataset: Albacore tuna, Bigeye tuna, Yellowfin tuna, Mahi Mahi, Opah, Sharks, Other (meaning that there are fish present but not in the above categories), and No Fish (meaning that no fish is in the picture). Each image has only one fish category, except that there are sometimes very small fish in the pictures that are used as bait.

The dataset was compiled by The Nature Conservancy in partnership with Satlink, Archipelago Marine Research, the Pacific Community, the Solomon Islands Ministry of Fisheries and Marine Resources, the Australia Fisheries Management Authority, and the governments of New Caledonia and Palau.

Files
- train.zip - zipped folder of all train images. The train folders are organized by fish species labels.
- test_stg1.zip - zipped folder of all test images in stage 1
- test_stg2.zip - zipped folder of all test images in stage 2 (not available until the second stage of the competition)
- sample_submission_stg1.csv - a sample submission file in the correct format
- sample_submission_stg2.csv - a sample submission file in the correct format (not available until the second stage of the competition)

Available files:
  sample_submission_stg1.csv (175.0 KB)
  sample_submission_stg2.csv (1.3 MB)
  test_stg2.7z (1.2 GB)
  __MACOSX/ (1 items)
  test_stg1/ (1000 items)
  train/ (9 items)

The dataset contains images organized by vessel and trip, with corresponding labels indicating the fish species or activity type. Each image is provided as a JPEG file, and metadata includes trip identifiers and timestamps to support temporal analysis.

Challenge:
# Acquire Valued Shoppers Challenge

## Competition Description
The Acquire Valued Shoppers Challenge invites participants to predict which customers are likely to become repeat purchasers after receiving an initial incentive. Using a comprehensive dataset of nearly 350 million transaction records from over 300,000 shoppers, competitors will analyze shopping behaviors to forecast long-term customer engagement. This task is crucial for retailers aiming to efficiently allocate marketing resources and build sustainable customer relationships.

## Evaluation
### Metric
Submissions are evaluated using the area under the ROC curve (AUC-ROC), which measures the ability of the predicted probabilities to correctly distinguish between customers who do and do not make repeat purchases. A higher AUC indicates better model performance in ranking customers by their likelihood of repeat purchase.

Data description:
Acquire Valued Shoppers Challenge

Dataset Description
Warning: this is a large data set. The decompressed files require about 22GB of space.

This data captures the process of offering incentives (a.k.a. coupons) to a large number of customers and forecasting those who will become loyal to the product. Let's say 100 customers are offered a discount to purchase two bottles of water. Of the 100 customers, 60 choose to redeem the offer. These 60 customers are the focus of this competition. You are asked to predict which of the 60 will return (during or after the promotional period) to purchase the same item again.

To create this prediction, you are given a minimum of a year of shopping history prior to each customer's incentive, as well as the purchase histories of many other shoppers (some of whom will have received the same offer). The transaction history contains all items purchased, not just items related to the offer. Only one offer per customer is included in the data. The training set is comprised of offers issued before 2013-05-01. The test set is offers issued on or after 2013-05-01.

Files
You are provided four relational files:

- transactions.csv - contains transaction history for all customers for a period of at least 1 year prior to their offered incentive
- trainHistory.csv - contains the incentive offered to each customer and information about the behavioral response to the offer
- testHistory.csv - contains the incentive offered to each customer but does not include their response (you are predicting the repeater column for each id in this file)
- offers.csv - contains information about the offers

Available files:
  offers.csv.gz (431 B)
  sampleSubmission.csv.gz (455.2 KB)
  testHistory.csv.gz (930.4 KB)
  trainHistory.csv.gz (1.0 MB)
  transactions.csv.gz (3.1 GB)

The dataset includes transaction-level details, customer histories, and promotional interactions, with key files such as testHistory.csv for prediction targets and sampleSubmission.csv outlining the required format. Each row in the test set corresponds to a unique customer for whom a repeat purchase probability must be estimated.
